Patricia, your talk has great data, but I am worried the audience will get lost in the detail.
Good point. I will start with a quick overview and a clear agenda, so they know the structure.
That helps. And how will you move between the sections?
With clear signposts. Firstly the framework, then a smooth transition to the findings.
And the ending? People only remember the last minute.
To summarize, I will recap the key points and emphasize one clear takeaway.
Let me start with a quick overview and the agenda.

To summarize, the main takeaway is structure.

Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the Abbott research session. Thank you for being here. Let me start with a quick overview of my presentation. Firstly, I will give you the agenda for today and explain the structure of the talk. Secondly, I will present our research framework and the methods we used. After that, I will move on to the main findings on glycemic control. Then, I will discuss what these results mean for clinical practice. Finally, I will recap the key points and share the most important takeaway. The whole presentation should take about twenty minutes, and I will leave time for questions at the end. So, let me begin with a short outline. There are three sections, and each one builds on the previous one. Please feel free to follow the slides, and do not worry about taking notes, because I will send the full deck after the session.

Now, let me move on to my final section and summarize what we have seen today. To recap, our study had three main goals. Firstly, we wanted to test a new framework for measuring glycemic control. Secondly, we wanted to compare it with the standard method. And finally, we wanted to understand the impact on patients in a real clinical setting. In short, the results were very positive. The new framework was more accurate, and patients found it easier to follow. If I had to choose one takeaway from this presentation, it would be this: a clear structure helps both the researcher and the audience. To summarize, structure is not a detail. It is the foundation of a good scientific talk. Thank you very much for your attention, and I look forward to your questions.

First, give a short overview and present the agenda. Next, explain your framework and your methods. After that, move on to the main findings. Then, discuss what the results mean. Finally, recap the key points and share the main takeaway.
